content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

June 21, 1989

Honorable Joseph G. Marsano
Watsonville City Councilmember
P.O. Box 202
Watsonville, CA 95076

Re: Your Request for a Confirmation
of Telephone Advice
Our File No. A-89-308

Dear Councilmember Marsano:

This is in response to your request for confirmation of
telephone advice regarding your duties under the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/

This letter confirms that your letter dated May 16, 1989 ac-
curately summarizes the telephone advice I provided to you on May
8, 1989. As I stated in our telephone conversation, Section 87100
prohibits any public official from participating in a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest. Section
87103(b) defines financial interest to include any real property
in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest
worth one thousand dollars ($1000) or more. Section 82033 further
defines an interest in real property to include an option to
acquire real property.

Black's Law Dictionary (4th Ed., 1968.) defines option as "A
privilege existing in one person, for which he has paid money,
which gives him the right to buy certain merchandise...from
another person, if he chooses, at any time within an agreed
period, at a fixed price...." Under contract law an option is
legally enforceable. (Riverside Fence Co. v. Novak (1969) 273 Cal.
App. 2d. 656, 663.)

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory refer-
ences are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Com-
mission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to
Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-89-308
Page 2

In our telephone conversation you stated you had a reservation on a home in a subdivision. The reservation was an unwritten, informal agreement by which you had the first opportunity to purchase the home. You stated that no money had changed hands and that you could withdraw the reservation without any penalty. The final map of the subdivision is now before the city council and you asked whether you could participate.

I concluded that a reservation on a home was not a legally enforceable option. A legal option to purchase real property must be in writing pursuant to the statute of frauds. (Civil Code Section 1624(c).) In addition, without some monetary consideration, the developer's promise to sell the home may be revoked. (Prather v. Vasquez (1958) 162 Cal. App. 2d 198, 204.) Thus, I advised that you do not have a real property interest in the subdivision that is the subject of the city council decision. I further advised that you could participate in the decision on the subdivision's final map, provided there was no other direct financial effect on you, and that you local conflict of interest provisions permitted participation.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter, please feel free to contact this office at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

KED:JWW:plh

Enclosures

FPPC
May 22 9:15 AM '89

May 16, 1989


Mr. John Wallace
Legal Division
Fair Political Practice Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804-0807

Dear Mr. Wallace:

     Pursuant to our conversation of May 8, 1989, please be advised
that I would like a written opinion on the conclusion of our conversation
concerning my participation on voting on the final map on a subdivision
in which I have a reservation on a lot during our May 9th city
council meeting.

     Please forward your response to the address given below. If
you have any questions please feel free in phoning the undersigned.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Joseph G. Marsano Jr.
P.O. Box 202
Watsonville Ca 95076

Phone: 408-724-7175

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 23, 1989


Joseph G. Marsano Jr.
P.O. Box 202
Watsonville, CA  95076

Re:  Letter No. 89-308

Dear Mr. Marsano:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act
was received on May 22, 1989 by the Fair Political Practices
Commission.  If you have any questions about your advice request,
you may contact John Wallace an attorney in the Legal Division,
directly at (916) 322-5501.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.  Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21
working days if your request seeks formal written advice.  If more
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to
information needed.  If your request is for informal assistance,
we will answer it as quickly as we can.  (See Commission
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).)

     You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,


Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

KED:plh


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

sections.conclusion: I concluded that a reservation on a home was not a legally enforceable option. A legal option to purchase real property must be in writing pursuant to the statute of frauds. (Civil Code Section 1624(c).) In addition, without some monetary consideration, the developer's promise to sell the home may be revoked. (Prather v. Vasquez (1958) 162 Cal. App. 2d 198, 204.) Thus, I advised you that you do not have a real property interest in the subdivision that is the subject of the city council decision. I further advised that you could participate in the decision on the subdivision's final map, provided there was no other direct financial effect on you, and that you local conflict of interest provisions permitted participation.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city councilmember who holds an unwritten, informal reservation on a home in a subdivision can participate in voting on the subdivision's final map without violating conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that an unwritten, informal reservation on a home is not a legally enforceable option to purchase real property because it lacks the writing required by the statute of frauds and lacks monetary consideration. Therefore, the councilmember does not have a financial interest that would prohibit participation in the vote, provided there is no other direct financial effect and local provisions permit participation.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is FPPC's confirmation letter of prior telephone advice dated June 21, 1989. Includes ancillary correspondence: recipient's request letter (May 16, 1989) and FPPC receipt acknowledgment (May 23, 1989). No separate QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections in formal Q&A format; advice is embedded in narrative. High confidence due to clear identification as advice letter confirming telephone consultation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a city councilmember who holds an unwritten, informal reservation on a home in a subdivision can participate in voting on the subdivision's final map without violating conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: I concluded that a reservation on a home was not a legally enforceable option. A legal option to purchase real property must be in writing pursuant to the statute of frauds. (Civil Code Section 1624(c).) In addition, without some monetary consideration, the developer's promise to sell the home may be revoked. (Prather v. Vasquez (1958) 162 Cal. App. 2d 198, 204.) Thus, I advised you that you do not have a real property interest in the subdivision that is the subject of the city council decision. I further advised that you could participate in the decision on the subdivision's final map, provided there was no other direct financial effect on you, and that you local conflict of interest provisions permitted participation.

embedding.summary: FPPC General Counsel Kathryn Donovan confirms prior telephone advice to Councilmember Marsano that his informal, unwritten reservation on a home in a subdivision does not constitute a legally enforceable real property interest sufficient to trigger conflict-of-interest restrictions under the Political Reform Act, allowing him to participate in voting on the subdivision's final map.
